story:
My right hand won’t stop shaking after the young Marshal arrives. I call it age, but I know better. Thomas Hartwell comes in with a notebook and the eyes of an old wolf. He asks about the body at the White Crow Mine. I give him fifteen years of practiced routine and a fresh lie: I was making rounds when Sarah Jenkins found me, screaming, said there was a dead man at the mine.

He writes without looking up. Time. Details. I say the dead man was James Morrison, a “merchant” from back east. Hartwell cuts into the cover: wrong season for cattle, strange for a merchant to be so armed and rich. I claim there was a Colt with pearl grip, lots of cash—only the wallet was empty when I found it. He notes every crack. He closes the net little by little. He asks about tracks, a struggle, a stranger with a scar and a black hat in the saloon. He asks why I visit an abandoned mine two or three times a week.

I say I watch for smuggling. He says, Of what? I float whiskey and stolen goods. He says guns. Winchester rifles. Bound for outlaw gangs. I sweat and say I didn’t know. He points out that I’ve been “investigating” a place where armed men ride at night, yet never found a thing.

Then he drops it: James Morrison wasn’t a merchant. He was a U.S. Marshal, undercover, here to track the gun trade. He had sent a report about “suspicious activity involving local authorities” and planned to search the mine the next morning—the morning he was killed. Hartwell says Morrison had kept his distance from me for exactly that reason. He asks me to accompany him to the mine now. It’s getting dark, I say. He answers that an innocent man isn’t afraid of the dark.

On the porch he gives me the rest, piece by piece. Sarah Jenkins never found the body; I coached her to say so. No scarred stranger exists; no one in town saw him. I described Morrison’s wounds too well—angle, distance, powder burn—details only someone very close would know. And there’s been an informant inside the operation who told them enough: weekly shipments, the mine depot, and that local law might be involved. Not names. Not yet.

I feel the ground give way. I’d dragged Morrison’s body into the mine, staged a struggle, built a story with churned dirt and displaced rocks, counted on a stranger no one could find and a town too scared to question. I thought I’d killed him before he could speak. Truth is, he was still warming up. If I’d kept my head, he might’ve left with suspicions and nothing more. Instead, I gave myself away—first with the murder, then with the lies. The empty wallet. The gun “kept as evidence.” The frequent trips to an “abandoned” mine. Word by word, contradiction by contradiction.

Hartwell puts on his hat and says the words I knew were coming: I’m under arrest for murder and for running a federal gun-smuggling ring. Wind hisses through the street. Somewhere, horses thunder—maybe my men fleeing with Winchesters, maybe already in irons. Fifteen years as a respected sheriff undone by fear. An experienced Marshal dead for nothing. And Hartwell? He barely had to push. I did his work for him.
